chunk_summary: This section outlines age-specific energy targets for complementary feeding, emphasizing meal frequency, energy density, and practical strategies to address energy gaps in breastfed and non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific energy requirements for breastfed and non-breastfed children aged 6–24 months.
Learn practical strategies to optimize energy density in complementary foods.
Recognize the importance of meal frequency and nutrient-dense feeding to prevent growth faltering.
Apply portion counselling techniques based on stomach capacity and energy density.
key_facts: Breastfed infants require complementary food energy of ~200 kcal/day at 6–8 months, ~300 kcal/day at 9–11 months, and ~550 kcal/day at 12–23 months.
Non-breastfed children need ~600 kcal/day at 6–8 months, ~700 kcal/day at 9–11 months, and ~900 kcal/day at 12–23 months from food and milk.
Energy density of meals should be ~0.8–1.0 kcal/g; lower densities (~0.6 kcal/g or less) require adjustments like adding oil, ghee, or nutrient-dense ingredients.
Portion sizes can be guided by stomach capacity, approximately 30 mL/kg per meal.
Thin, watery foods like rice water or dal water are insufficient and can lead to growth faltering.
reference_quote: For breastfed infants, the energy that must come from complementary foods rises across age bands: about 200 kcal/day at 6–8 months, 300 kcal/day at 9–11 months, and 550 kcal/day at 12–23 months (in addition to breast milk).
document_key_topics: Age-specific energy requirements for complementary feeding
Meal frequency and portion counselling strategies
Preventing growth faltering through nutrient-dense feeding
